pieter-bruegel-the-elder-the-harvesters: August in Flanders. Bruegel cut the painting horizontally — sky, wheat, foreground — and let ordinary labor fill the lower band. The man asleep with his legs splayed under the pear tree is one of the great unceremonial figures in Western painting. Part of a series of months commissioned by an Antwerp merchant; only five panels survive.
caravaggio-michelangelo-merisi-the-music: An early commission for Cardinal del Monte, painted soon after Caravaggio arrived in Rome. The boys are tuning, not performing — the pause before the note. The figure at left holding a cornetto is a self-portrait. The crowded composition and the bared shoulder reveal an artist still working out how to stage drama in paint; within a few years he would have it.
el-greco-domenikos-theotokopoulos-view-o: One of two surviving landscapes by El Greco — the other a topographical map. Here, no map. The Tagus has been moved, the cathedral relocated, the hill compressed into a charged silhouette. The sky does the work of an emotion. Painted around 1600, it took three centuries before the modernists understood what he was doing.
johannes-vermeer-young-woman-with-a-wate: A morning in Delft. The window is open, the pitcher cool, the woman caught between two intentions. Vermeer reduces the scene to a small set of objects and lets the light describe them. Note how the brass pitcher reflects both blue from the cloth and gold from the window — a single vessel doing the work of the whole room.
rembrandt-rembrandt-van-rijn-aristotle-w: Commissioned by a Sicilian collector who had never met Rembrandt. The philosopher rests his hand on the blind poet — the living touching the dead, knowledge touching its source. The gold chain marks worldly success; Aristotle's gaze suggests he isn't sure it was worth it. One of the most psychologically charged paintings of the seventeenth century.
jacques-louis-david-the-death-of-socrate: Painted on the eve of the French Revolution. Socrates points to the heavens, accepts the hemlock, and continues his lecture. David strips the room to its essentials — stone, cloth, gesture — and stages philosophy as theater. Reynolds called it the greatest work since the Sistine Chapel; it became the visual handbook for Neoclassicism and, soon after, for revolution itself.
katsushika-hokusai-under-the-wave-off-ka: The most reproduced image in the world, and still arresting. Hokusai uses Prussian blue — recently arrived in Japan from Europe — and a printmaker's economy of line. The boats are not victims; they are part of the wave's geometry. Mount Fuji, small and white, is the still point.
joseph-mallord-william-turner-whalers: Late Turner: form dissolving into weather. The whale is barely there — a smudge, a suggestion — and the ship is being eaten by its own atmosphere. Painted at a moment when Turner was essentially inventing what painting would do for the next century. Critics in 1845 thought he had lost it. He hadn't.
utagawa-hiroshige-plum-garden-at-kamata: A late print from One Hundred Famous Views of Edo, made the year before Hiroshige's death. The vertical composition — branches in the foreground, garden in the middle distance, sky at the top — would directly influence Van Gogh, who copied a similar Hiroshige in oils. The pink is bokashi, a hand-applied gradient achieved with a damp brush on the woodblock.
edouard-manet-boating: Painted at Argenteuil in the summer of 1874, the year of the first Impressionist exhibition. Manet refuses depth — the water is flat, the boat is flat, the woman's white dress is a single shape. Henry James called the painting 'the last word in painting.' It is in fact one of the first words of modern painting.
edgar-degas-the-dancing-class: Not a performance — a rehearsal. Degas was obsessed with the unposed body: the scratch, the stretch, the wait. Jules Perrot, the ballet master, leans on his stick at the center. The girls are at various stages of attention. Degas made dozens of these; he understood that the moment before the moment is where character lives.
paul-cezanne-the-card-players: One of five versions; this is the smallest and tightest. Two peasants face each other across a table — that is the entire composition. Cézanne removes everything theatrical and leaves only structure: the geometry of shoulders, the architecture of the bottle, the deliberate weight of each hand. Picasso and Braque studied these paintings carefully; Cubism began here.
vincent-van-gogh-wheat-field-with-cypres: Painted in June 1889 from the asylum at Saint-Rémy-de-Provence. Van Gogh wrote to his brother Theo about the cypresses: 'beautiful as regards lines and proportions, like an Egyptian obelisk.' The paint is pushed, dragged, twisted — the wind made visible. He completed three versions; this is the one he considered the best of the summer.
john-singer-sargent-madame-x-virginie-am: The painting that nearly ended Sargent's career. When it appeared in the Salon of 1884 with the right strap fallen off Madame Gautreau's shoulder, Paris was scandalized; her family demanded the painting's withdrawal. Sargent repainted the strap and left for London. He kept the painting in his studio for thirty years before selling it to the Met, calling it 'the best thing I have done.'
gustav-klimt-mada-primavesi-1903-2000: Mäda was nine. Her father was Klimt's patron in Vienna. The dress is a wall of pattern; the carpet beneath her is another. Only the face and the planted feet are still — and they are the entire painting. Klimt understood that a child standing her ground is more interesting than any adult posed for one.
